Good evening, welcome to The Meridian. May I have your name, please?
It's Ito, I-T-O. You may have it under Yto -- there's often a mix-up. Could you tell me if there's a reservation for two nights, arriving today?
Ah, there it is -- my apologies for the spelling. I have you in room three-oh-four, first floor, facing the street. Here's your key card.
Thank you. I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher floor, somewhere a little quieter. Do you know whether anything is available facing the back?
Let me check. Yes -- I have a room on the fourteenth floor, facing the courtyard, at the same rate for double occupancy. Shall I move you there?
That would be perfect. One more thing -- the last room I saw had a faulty air conditioner. Would you mind making sure the new one works?
Of course. An engineer will check it before you go up. Is there anything else I can help you with tonight?
Yes -- I was wondering if I could extend my stay by one night, and settle the whole bill when I leave on Sunday.
It's Ito, I-T-O. Could you tell me if there's a reservation under that name?
I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher, quieter floor.
I'm sorry to bother you, but the air conditioning seems to be faulty.
Do you know if breakfast is included in the rate?
Could you tell me if I could extend my stay and settle the bill on Sunday?

Good evening, Ms. Ito, this is Marcus calling from the front desk. I'm just following up on the requests you made when you checked in, so everything is ready for you. First, the room. You asked whether we could move you to a higher, quieter floor, away from the street, and we were happy to do that. You are now in room fourteen-oh-two, on the fourteenth floor, facing the courtyard. Your new key card is waiting for you at the desk whenever you come down. Second, the air conditioning. You mentioned that the unit in your first room was faulty and would not cool down. An engineer has already checked the new room, so that is taken care of, but if anything feels wrong, please just let me know and someone will be sent up right away. Third, you asked earlier whether it would be possible to extend your stay by one night, until Sunday. I can confirm that the room is available, so your checkout has been moved to Sunday at noon, and everything will be added to one bill that you can settle when you leave. And finally, you wanted to know if we could set up an adjoining room for your colleague when she arrives on Thursday. We have one held next to yours. So, everything you asked for is arranged. If you need anything at all during your stay, please pick up the phone and dial zero. Enjoy your evening in New York, Ms. Ito.

Welcome back to The Careful Traveler, the show for people who want to feel at home anywhere in the world. Today, one small skill that changes every hotel stay: how to ask for what you want without sounding rude, and without freezing up. Here is the problem. In your own language, you know exactly how to be polite. But in English, under pressure, at a busy front desk, many people go too direct. They say, give me another room, or, where is the elevator, and it comes out sharp, even when they are lovely people. There is a simple fix, and once you have it, you will use it everywhere. It is called the indirect question. Instead of asking directly, you put a soft opener in front. Watch what happens. Where is the gym becomes could you tell me where the gym is. Is breakfast included becomes do you know if breakfast is included. Can I have a late checkout becomes I was wondering if it would be possible to have a late checkout. Notice two things. First, the opener does all the polite work: could you tell me, do you know, I was wondering if, would it be possible to. Second, and this is where everyone slips, after the opener the sentence goes back to normal order. It is where the gym is, not where is the gym. It is if breakfast is included, not is breakfast included. The question mark moves to the front, and the rest relaxes. Here is your challenge for your next trip. Every time you want to ask for something at the front desk, start with could you tell me or I was wondering if. A better room, a quieter floor, a later checkout, a fix for a faulty air conditioner. You are not asking for less. You are asking in a way that makes the person on the other side want to help you. And that, everywhere in the world, is the whole game.
